nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; inhabits coral-rich areas of clear lagoons and seaward reefs at depths of roughly 1-30 m. Adults are typically solitary and shelter near or beneath ledges and caves by day, foraging over adjacent sand and rubble at night. Juveniles occupy coral-rich shallows.

decorPreferences: live rock arranged into caves and ledges

diet.notes: In the wild has been documented feeding nocturnally on crustaceans, mollusks, and small fishes. Newly imported specimens are notoriously reluctant feeders and have been reported to fast for extended periods; live saltwater feeder shrimp (or freshwater ghost shrimp) are commonly used to entice initial feeding. Once acclimated, generally accepts a varied diet of meaty marine foods including chopped shrimp, squid, clams, mussel, and marine fish flesh, along with prepared frozen carnivore foods. Multiple small feedings per day are typically recommended to support its rapid growth.

avoidWith: very small fish that could be swallowed
highly aggressive tankmates (juveniles are easily stressed)

imageCandidates.notes: Rickard Zerpe; 5184x3888. Tack-sharp in-situ juvenile showing the diagnostic dark-brown body with large white-margined spots (flatworm-mimic phase); vibrant, whole fish framed over coral.
Rickard Zerpe; 3959x2969. Sharp side view of a subadult in the transitional phase with bold brown/white blotches and heavily spotted fins; illustrates the mid-metamorphosis look.
Jens Petersen; 3264x2448. Aggregation of adults showing the terminal color form (pale body densely covered in dark spots); documents the dramatic adult phase noted in the summary.

summary: The Spotted Sweetlips (Plectorhinchus chaetodonoides) is a large, peaceful Indo-Pacific fish that grows to around 60–72 cm and undergoes a dramatic juvenile-to-adult color change. It is best suited to very large fish-only systems and is generally recommended for expert aquarists.
careNotes:
The Spotted Sweetlips, also widely sold as Harlequin or Clown Sweetlips, is one of the more challenging large reef fish in the hobby. It is generally regarded as peaceful toward unrelated tankmates but its eventual size (roughly 60-72 cm) and rapid growth rate make it impractical for all but the largest private systems; it is most commonly recommended only for advanced aquarists or public aquariums.

A defining trait is its juvenile-to-adult color metamorphosis: small juveniles are dark brown with large white-margined spots and a wobbly, flatworm-like swimming gait that is thought to mimic toxic flatworms as predator deterrence. As the fish matures, the pattern inverts to a pale body covered in dark spots, and the swimming style normalises. Buyers should be aware that the small spotted juvenile they purchase will look very different at adult size.

Feeding is the most commonly cited husbandry challenge. Newly imported individuals frequently refuse food for extended periods and have been reported to starve in captivity if not enticed promptly. Live saltwater feeder shrimp (or freshwater ghost shrimp as a stopgap) are the conventional starter food; once feeding, the fish generally transitions to chopped shrimp, squid, clams, mussel meat, marine fish flesh, and prepared frozen carnivore foods. Multiple small feedings per day are commonly advised given the rapid growth rate.

Reef compatibility is rated 'with caution': the species is not aggressive but will hunt smaller crustaceans, fireworms, serpent stars, small snails, and polychaete worms, making it generally unsuitable for typical reef setups. Fish-only-with-live-rock systems featuring caves and ledges for daytime shelter and large open swimming areas are preferred. Like other Haemulidae, this species produces audible grunting sounds via pharyngeal teeth.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been documented in home aquaria.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Spotted Sweetlips product page (https://www.liveaquaria.com/products/spotted-sweetlips) provided care level Expert Only, max size 29 in (~74 cm), minimum tank 500+ gal (~1893 L), temperament Peaceful, reef-safe With Caution, temperature 72-78°F (22.2-25.6°C), pH 8.1-8.4, dKH 8-12, SG 1.020-1.025, native origin Indonesia, color form Black/Tan/White as adult with juvenile brown/white-spotted phase, diet Carnivore with live-shrimp initiation guidance.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed family Haemulidae, order Eupercaria/misc, max TL 72 cm (common SL 60 cm), depth 1-30 m, Indo-West Pacific distribution (Maldives to Fiji, Ryukyus to Rowley Shoals), reef-associated marine/brackish habitat, preferred temperature 25.5-29.3°C, nocturnal carnivore on crustaceans/mollusks/fishes, oviparous with distinct pairing, juvenile flatworm-mimicry note.

additional.name: reefapp.net Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked tank size and reef safety: minimum tank volume 2000 L (~528 gal), maximum size 72 cm, salinity SG ~1.026, care rating 'experience and preparation required', temperament docile/shy, reef-safe with caution. Lifespan figure (~15 years) drawn from supplementary hobby-aquarium sources. Reef2Reef wiki secondary returned HTTP 403; reefapp.net substituted to give a third confirming source consistent with the Stage C playbook secondary-fallback rule.

fish.breedingNotes: Wild spawning is oviparous, with adults forming distinct pairs to spawn. Captive reproduction has not been documented for typical home aquaria, given the species' adult size.
